title: QA: guardrails check_file_access fails open on Mutex poison/None state (guardrails.rs)
description:
## Severity
Major
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/guardrails.rs:43-50, 26-35
## Violation
The blast-radius check returns `Ok(())` whenever the global guardrails
state cannot be inspected — both when the mutex is poisoned and when the
engine was never configured:
```rust
pub fn check_file_access(path: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
    if let Ok(guard) = GUARDRAILS.lock() {
        if let Some(engine) = guard.as_ref() {
            return engine.check_file_access(path);
        }
    }
    Ok(())
}
```
The `if let Ok` swallows `PoisonError`; the inner `if let Some` covers
the "never configured" case. Either way the function returns success and
the caller proceeds to write `.git/config` or `/etc/shadow`. This is a
classic fail-open security failure (OWASP A04 Insecure Design).
`configure()` (lines 30-35) silently no-ops on poison as well.
## Mandated refactor
Distinguish three states explicitly: `Disabled`, `Enabled(engine)`,
`Poisoned`. Return `Err("guardrails poisoned — refusing write")` on
poison. Either require explicit configuration before any tool dispatches,
or return a typed `GuardrailDecision { Unconfigured, Allowed, Denied(...) }`
and force the caller to handle `Unconfigured` (fail-closed for write
tools). Replace `if let Ok(guard) = ...lock()` with
`.lock().expect("guardrails poisoned")` if poison genuinely should crash
the process.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
